data.title: Soybean barge basis steady-up on good demand, firm freight
data.description:
Basis bids for soybeans shipped by barge to U.S. Gulf Coast terminals were steady to higher on Tuesday on good exporter demand and firmer freight costs, while corn basis bids were narrowly mixed, traders said.

  • Spot barge freight rates were steady to higher on Tuesday as low water on the Mississippi River and ice buildup on the Illinois River slowed navigation.

  • Gulf exporters are actively shipping U.S. soybeans as the harvest in Brazil begins to accelerate. Brazilian soybeans are offered a discount to U.S. soybeans in March and beyond.

  • Chinese purchases of U.S. soybeans are broadly expected to slow after Beijing appeared to reach a U.S.-stated target of buying 12 million metric tons from the latest harvest.

  • The U.S. Department of Agriculture said 1,336,684 tons of U.S. soybeans were inspected for export last week, up 35% from the same week a year ago. The loadings included nine vessels of soybeans to China, according to USDA data.

  • Exporters are monitoring tensions between the United States and Europe over U.S. President Trump's demands to control Greenland. Trump has threatened tariffs on several European countries, igniting concerns of a renewed trade war with a key market for U.S. corn and soybeans.

  • CIF bids for Gulf soybean barges loaded in January were up 3 cents at 102 cents over Chicago Board of Trade March soybean (SH26) futures. February barge bids were up 1 cent at 99 cents over futures.

  • FOB Gulf soybean offers for vessels loaded in February were steady at 118 cents over March futures, while offers for March shipments were steady at 115 cents over futures.

  • CIF Gulf January corn barges were bid 2 cents lower at 86 cents over CBOT March corn (CH26) futures and February corn barges were bid at steady at 91 cents over March futures.

  • FOB export premiums for February corn shipments were 107 cents over March futures and March shipments were offered at 111 cents over March futures, both steady.

data.title: Minnesota leaders subpoenaed in US criminal probe over opposition to immigration crackdown
data.description:
Key points:
  • Offices of six state and local officials are targeted
  • Surge of federal agents in Twin Cities has sparked protests
  • Minnesota Democrats call investigation politically motivated

By Maria Alejandra Cardona, Jana Winter and Andrew Hay

The U.S. Justice Department on Tuesday subpoenaed the offices of Minnesota's governor and attorney general, and mayors of Minneapolis and St. Paul, as it weighed whether their public opposition to President Donald Trump's immigration enforcement surge in the Twin Cities amounts to a crime.

One of the jury subpoenas, shared with the media by Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey, orders his office's custodian of records to produce documents since the beginning of 2025 related to "cooperation or lack of cooperation with federal immigration authorities."

The federal grand jury subpoenas were served on six offices of state and local Democrats, according to a Justice Department official, including those of Governor Tim Walz and Attorney General Keith Ellison.

WALZ, FREY SAY PUBLIC AT RISK

Trump, a Republican, has sent thousands of Border Patrol and Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents into the Minneapolis area in recent weeks to conduct deportation roundups, unprecedented in scale, that have led to numerous violent encounters with residents.

The agents have carried rifles through the city's snowy streets, dressed in military-style camouflage, tactical gear and masks, drawing loud but mostly peaceful protests from residents.

Walz and Frey have denounced the ICE operations as reckless political theater that was putting the public at risk and was designed to provoke chaos that Trump would use as a pretext to exert an even greater show of force.

Although he has urged protesters to remain orderly, Walz also has openly encouraged citizens to record video of any arrests or other encounters between ICE agents and members of the public to create a database for potential "future prosecution" of wrongdoing by federal law enforcement.

Trump administration officials have accused Walz and Frey of deliberately stoking interference with ICE operations in "collusion" with anti-government agitators, which the governor denies. Justice Department officials did not immediately respond to requests for further comment.

The hostility of many residents to the ICE crackdown has only deepened since one agent fatally shot an American woman, Renee Good, in her car nearly two weeks ago.

Federal officers have used tear gas and other chemical irritants against protesters, and have drawn outrage for racially profiling Black, Latino and Asian U.S. citizens, including a man who was wrongly arrested and pulled out of his home on Sunday dressed in underpants and sandals.

The subpoenas arrived a few days after it became public that the U.S. Justice Department had opened a criminal investigation of Walz, Frey and other Democrats and outspoken critics of Trump's deportation drive in Minnesota.

Walz, who unsuccessfully ran for vice president in the 2024 election won by Trump, said the federal justice system was being weaponized to intimidate Trump's political adversaries.

He has pointed to investigations opened in recent weeks against such figures as Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, whom Trump has criticized as being too hesitant to lower interest rates, and several Democratic lawmakers who previously served in the armed forces, and issued a video statement urging members of the military to resist illegal orders.

It would be highly unusual for federal prosecutors to bring a criminal conspiracy case based on statements from public officials about government policies.

The Justice Department has struggled in some cases to secure indictments from grand juries, an unusual rebuke given that prosecutors alone control the presentation of evidence and must only show probable cause that a crime was committed, a lower legal standard than to obtain a conviction at trial.

Grand juries twice rejected the DOJ's attempts to re-indict New York Attorney General Letitia James after a judge threw out a criminal case against her and also refused to sign off on several cases connected to Trump's law enforcement surge in Washington last summer.

DEMOCRATS HAVE CALLED FOR CALM

Democratic politicians in Minnesota have also sued the Trump administration, asking a judge last week to order an end to what they call the unconstitutional excesses of his enforcement surge.

On Friday, a federal judge in Minnesota barred immigration agents deployed en masse in the Minneapolis area from arresting, detaining or using pepper spray and other crowd-control munitions against peaceful protesters or individuals who were merely observing ICE activities.

The injunction was granted in response to a lawsuit brought on behalf of a group of citizens who said their constitutional rights had been infringed by federal agents.

"Minnesotans are more concerned with safety and peace rather than with baseless legal tactics aimed at intimidating public servants standing shoulder to shoulder with their community," Walz said in a statement on Friday.

Frey said in a separate statement that the federal government was trying "to intimidate local leaders for doing their jobs" and that "every American should be concerned."

"We shouldn't live in a country where federal law enforcement is used to play politics or crack down on local voices they disagree with," he said.

data.title: Protein premiums firm; wintry weather eyed
data.description:
Basis bids for hard red winter (HRW) wheat held steady at grain elevators in the southern U.S. Plains on Tuesday, spot checks showed, while protein premiums were flat to higher, typically a sign of demand from domestic flour mills.

  • Protein premiums for railcar wheat to or through Kansas City firmed by 20 cents a bushel for HRW wheat with 11.6% through 12.4% protein, and by 5 cents for wheat with 12.6% protein.

  • Market players were eying forecasts for a winter storm that is expected to bring snow and sleet to the Southern Plains on Friday into Saturday, followed by frigid Arctic air that could threaten dormant HRW wheat.

  • Any precipitation could help insulate winter wheat from the cold, an Oklahoma merchandiser said.

  • But wheat in areas lacking snow cover, including Nebraska, could be vulnerable to freeze damage late this week, weather forecaster Vaisala said in a client note.

  • K.C. March HRW wheat futures (KWH26) settled down 4-1/4 cents on Tuesday at $5.23 a bushel.
